Re: 
Opinion Request 
No. 77-038 
Dear Mr. Lewis: 
Thank you for your letter to the Commiss 
da 
October 
8, 1977, requesting an opinion concerning ce tain 
disclosure and di 
lification responsibilities of a member 
of the Tahoe-Truckee Un i fied School Distr iet. 
> The Commission 
will not issue a formal opinion concerning the matters in 
question because your request does not raise substanti 
issues of interpretation under the Political Reform 
t 
("Act"). 
See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18329lb). 
However 
I 
trust the following informr7 advice issued pursuant to Govern-
ment Code Section 83114(b)-
will enable you and Dr. Black 
to resolve the matter in question. 
In i tially we discuss Dr. Black's reporting obI iga-
tions, as set out in questions one through three of your 
letter. 
Paramount to any discussion of Dr. Black's reporting 
obligations is the admonition that these duties must be 
viewed in the context of the relevant disclosure category to' 
which Dr. Black is ass 
ned under the Conflict of Interest 
Code promul gated 2,nd adopted by the Tahoe-Truckee Uni ied 
School District.-
Only sources of income fal ing within 
the relevant definition of "reportable income" need be dis-
closed by Dr. Black. 
Under the District Code disclosure for 
a school director, such as Dr. Black, is less comprehensive 
All statutory references are to 
Code unles otherwise noted.

than that required of certain public officials such as members 
of Boards of Supervisors or City Councils under Section 
87200. 
With this important caveat in mind, and assuming 
that some, but not all, of Dr. Black's "sources of income" 
would be reportable under the provisions of the Code, we now 
proceed to answer your questions. 
Because Dr. Black receives income from his medical 
practice, his practice is a source of income to him within 
the meaning of Sections 82030 and 87l03(c). 
In addition, 
Section 82030 defines "income" in relevant 
rtf to include: 
a pro rata share of any income of any business 
entity or trust in which the individual ••. owns 
••• a lO-percent interest or greater. 
Thus, along with Dr. Black's medi al 
act ce 
itself, certain patients of Dr. Black's who provide 
ncome 
his business enti 
medical practi e - will be a 
urce of income to h m. 
~owever, as you indicate in your 
1 tter only those sources of income to a bu iness entity 
that pay in excess of $10,000 in fees duri 
the applicable 
r 
rting period n 
be disclosed on a Statement of Economic 
Interests. 
This conclusion is dictated by 
tioD 8 207(b)(3) 
which provides in r 
evant part: 
(b) 
n income of a business entity, includ-
ing income of a sole proprieto 
p, is required 
to be reported under this article, the statement 
shall contain: 
(3) 
In the case of a business entity 
not covered by paragraph (2), the name of 
every person from whom the business entity 
received payments if the filer's pro rata 
share of gross receipts from such person was 
equal to or greater than ten thou~,nd doll rs 
($10,000) during a 
endar 
ar.-
with res 
ct to your third question, it is our 
conclusion 
t 
t 
patient, not the patient's 
nsurance 
carrier, is the source of income to Dr. Black. 
The policy 
e Conflict of Inte est Cod 
of the 
Sc 
1 Distri t contains 1 
uage 
ns. 


Mr. Douqlas A. Lewis 
December 27, 1977 
Page Three 
underlying disclosure of certain sources of income under the 
Act and Conflict of Interest Codes adopted pursuant thereto 
is stated in Section 81002(d). 
That section provides: 
(d) 
Assets and income of public officials 
which may be materially affected 
their official 
actions 
uld be disclosed and in appropriate 
circumstances the officials should be disqualified 
from acting in order that conflicts of interest 
may be avoided: 
We believe this policy envisions the existence of 
potential conflicts of interest in those cases in which 
certain economic relationships may interfere with a public 
official's exercise of objective judgment in making govern-
mental decis 
os. 
In this case, most decisions to enter 
into an economic relationship with the 
tor would be made 
by the patient, not the insurance carrier. 
Since the decision 
to enter into the relationship is made by t 
patient and 
not the insurance company, it is not likely that the payment 
by the i surance com 
on behalf of the 
tient will in-
fluence the doctor' 
government decis ons 
i 
may affect 
t 
insurance 
Accordingly, Dr. Black should report the names of 
those patients otherwise reportable under the Code who, 
along with their insurance companies, paid Dr. Black fees in 
excess of $10,000 during the reporting period. 
The exception 
to this general rule would be if the insurer, or some other 
person or business ent ty sent patients to 
. Black pursuant 
to some formal agreement or plan. 
Under such circumstances, 
we might reach a different conclusion, but it is our under-
standing that this is not the case in the instant situation. 
We turn now to your final question which aSK!1 
whether Dr. Black in light of Sections 87100 and 87103-1 
(and the disqualification provisions of the School District's 
Those sections provide that: 
87100. 
No public official at any level of state 
or local government shall make, participate in 
making or in any way attempt to use his official 
position to influence a governmental decision in 
ieh he knows or has reason to know he has a 
f nancial interest. 
(continued next page) 


Mr. Douglas A. Lewis 
December 27, 1977 
Page Four 
Conflict of Interest Code) must disqualify himself 
om 
pa~ticipation in decisions of the Board relating to health 
and welfare benefits received by employees of the District 
if one or more of the employees of the District are also his 
patients. 
As indicated, above, under the terms of the Act, 
both Dr. Blackts medical practice and certain patients of 
that practice will be "sources of income" to him within the 
meaning of Section 87l03(c), su~ra. Thus, it is necessary 
to analyze the effect the Board s decisions concerning he 
th 
and welfare benefits of employees will have on those various 
sources of income. 
As indicated above, the insurers of 
Dr. Black's patients generally are not sources of income to 
h 
Therefore, the analysis must center on the effect of 
the decision on his own practice (or his patients), not the 
effect of the decision on his patient's ins rance carriers 
There are two ways in which the Board's decision 
regarding health benefits might possibly affect Dr. Black's 
patients and practice. 
First, you have suggested that an 
insurance company might give Or. Black "preietential treat-
ment" if he selected or recommended a parti ular policy or 
plan for the employees of the District and 
hat such ~pref­
erential treatment" will have a 
reseeable material financial 
effect on his practice. 
In the absence of 
pecific facts, 
we think this hypothesis is so speculative as to not be a 
"foreseeable" consequence of the decision w thin the meaning 
of Section 87103. 
(lootnote 4 continued) 
87103. 
An official has a financial interest in a 
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it 
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will 
have a material financial effect, distinguishable 
from its effect on the public gen~rally, on: 
(c) 
Any source of income, other than loans 
by a commercial lending institution in the regular 
course of business, aggregating two hundred fifty 
dollars ($250) or more in value r ceived by or 
promised to the public official w thin twelve 
months prior to the time when the decision is 
made; ••• 


Mr. Douglas A. Lewis 
December 27, 1977 
Page Five 
Second, you suggested that Dr. Black's deci ions 
on health and welfare plans may affect his 
actice by in-
creasing or decreasing the number of patient 
who use his 
services. 
The fact that Dr. Black specializes in 
ac-
tures leads us to a conclusion that the majo ity of his 
decisions concerning 
a1th and we fare bene its will not 
have a 
reseeable materi 
financial effect on eithe 
his 
practice or those patients who constitute sources of income 
to him. 
It is unl ikel y tha t any person waul 
choose to 
cease or begin using Dr. Black's services merely beca se of 
his decisions concerni 
the 
a1th plan. 
It also does no 
appear probable t 
t 
t 
decision of the District would 
involve either including or exclud ng the 
th plan's 
coverage of bone fractures. 
Even if he did make a decision 
concerning bone fracture coverage there is no evidence to 
sugge~t tg;t the decision would materially a fectDr. Black's 
practlce.-
We would 
remiss if in the context of thi 
advice 
we did not raise one more 
tential issue not specifically 
addressed in your letter. 
Since some employees of the District 
are sources of income to Dr. Black, the ques ion might arise 
as to whether Dr. Bl 
may participate in decisions concerning 
matters such as employee wages, terms and conditions of 
employment, disciplinary actions, etc. 
We do not think that 
the Act should be read as prohibiting Dr. 
ack from partici-
pating in this wide range of Board actions. 
Support for 
this conclusion is garnered from the Commission's regulation 
defining "material financial effect," 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18702, a copy of which is enclosed. 
In·short, viewing the 
financial relationship of Dr. Black to his patients, we do 
not believe that in matters such as these the existence of 
the financial interest will interfere with Dr. Black's per-
formance of his duties in an impartial mann r free from 
bias. 
Accordingly, we conclude that 
probably will not be 
prohibited from participating in the bulk 0 
matter 
comi 
before the District Board. 
~/ Although there is no s 
9 sti n in this case 
that 
th plan decisions will affeo 
he 
octor's practice, 
t 
re may be cases 
ere a decis 
n to inor ase the s 
of 
health plan cover 
e would lead to a s 
sta tial increase in 
a doctor 5 business 
Such a situat 
n migh 
arise if, for 
example, it was foreseeable that the decis 
n of a 
ychiatr s 
school 
membe 
to exte 
a 
alth plan to inc I 
e 
iatric treatme t would result in 
n incre se in t 
n 
of persons seeki 
uch treatment.

December 27, 1977

Thank you again for writing. 
I trust this letter 
answers your questions. 
If you wish to appeal the denial 
your opinion request, you may do so pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. 
Code Section 18321. 
If we can be of further assistance in 
this or any other matter, please feel free to contact Kenneth 
Goshorn of the Conflicts of Interest Division at (916) 322-
6444.

Lewis Placer County Schools' Attorney 1230 High Street Auburn, CA 95603 Re: Opinion Request No. 77-038 Dear Mr. Lewis: Thank you for your letter to the Commiss da October 8, 1977, requesting an opinion concerning ce tain disclosure and di lification responsibilities of a member of the Tahoe-Truckee Un i fied School Distr iet. > The Commission will not issue a formal opinion concerning the matters in question because your request does not raise substanti issues of interpretation under the Political Reform t ("Act"). See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18329lb). However I trust the following informr7 advice issued pursuant to Govern- ment Code Section 83114(b)- will enable you and Dr. Black to resolve the matter in question. In i tially we discuss Dr. Black's reporting obI iga- tions, as set out in questions one through three of your letter. Paramount to any discussion of Dr. Black's reporting obligations is the admonition that these duties must be viewed in the context of the relevant disclosure category to' which Dr. Black is ass ned under the Conflict of Interest Code promul gated 2,nd adopted by the Tahoe-Truckee Uni ied School District.- Only sources of income fal ing within the relevant definition of "reportable income" need be dis- closed by Dr. Black. Under the District Code disclosure for a school director, such as Dr. Black, is less comprehensive All statutory references are to Code unles otherwise noted. Governmen t Distri t entiti materials t specif most of t We the y those i ions of the e 0 investments in iness in t ,ltt is 0 vi that iate t n r x: 1 et te Mr. Douglas A. Lewis December 27, 1977 Page Two than that required of certain public officials such as members of Boards of Supervisors or City Councils under Section 87200. With this important caveat in mind, and assuming that some, but not all, of Dr. Black's "sources of income" would be reportable under the provisions of the Code, we now proceed to answer your questions. Because Dr. Black receives income from his medical practice, his practice is a source of income to him within the meaning of Sections 82030 and 87l03(c). In addition, Section 82030 defines "income" in relevant rtf to include: a pro rata share of any income of any business entity or trust in which the individual ••. owns ••• a lO-percent interest or greater. Thus, along with Dr. Black's medi al act ce itself, certain patients of Dr. Black's who provide ncome his business enti medical practi e - will be a urce of income to h m. ~owever, as you indicate in your 1 tter only those sources of income to a bu iness entity that pay in excess of $10,000 in fees duri the applicable r rting period n be disclosed
